name: Berkeley Center for Independent Living
population_served: People with disabilities, cross-disability, initially focused on physically disabled college students
anti_capture_mechanisms: majority_disabled_governance: >51% of board and staff required to have disabilities — structural rule prevents non-disabled professional capture
peer_not_professional: peer counselors replace social workers and medical case managers — deliberately de-professionalizes care to resist clinical capture
anti_medical_model: explicitly frames disability as social/political problem not individual medical problem — blocks medical authority over service design
cross_disability_solidarity: serves all disability types, not condition-specific — prevents single-issue medical lobbying from capturing organizational frame
community_not_charity: designed as alternative to institutional control (state hospitals, nursing homes, rehab industry) not supplement to it — adversarial to capture vectors from founding
replicated_without_center: model spread to 400+ independent centers globally — no single killable node, each center locally governed
scale: Single Berkeley center at founding; ~400 CILs nationwide, ~500 globally as of 2022
outcome: Survived and replicated. ADA (1990) incorporated CIL infrastructure into federal policy — partial absorption. Anti-capture mechanisms held: CILs retained peer governance requirement. Federal funding through Rehabilitation Act Title VII added resource capture pressure; centers that took federal money faced compliance conditions. The structural majority-disabled requirement proved durable — no center can be captured by non-disabled professionals as long as the 51% rule holds. A formal organization, but one whose anti-capture architecture is load-bearing in its bylaws.
notes: Key case for structural anti-capture in formal organizations: the 51% disabled governance rule is anti-capture architecture written into bylaws. Compare to cooperative ownership requirement — same mechanism, different domain. ADA absorption created federal funding dependency but could not override the governance structure. This is partial absorption: form survived, but resource dependency creates ongoing capture pressure. Fifth question relevance: CIL confronted power (Rehab Act, ADA advocacy, nursing home industry) — not just demonstrating service but building toward policy confrontation. The breakfast program was the door; disability rights litigation was the point.
